data.content:
**Yes, C# fully supports `stackalloc`'d `Span` of native-sized integers.** Both `Span<nint>` and `Span<nuint>` work:

```csharp
Span<nint>  a = stackalloc nint[4];   // ✅ works
Span<nuint> b = stackalloc nuint[4];  // ✅ works
```

Tested on .NET 10.0 — compiled and ran without issues. On this 64-bit system, `nint.MaxValue` was `9223372036854775807` (Int64 max) and `nuint.MaxValue` was `18446744073709551615` (UInt64 max), confirming the native pointer size is 8 bytes.

This has been supported since **C# 9 / .NET 5**, when `nint` and `nuint` became first-class keyword aliases for `System.IntPtr` / `System.UIntPtr`.
